name: Albentosa - Arenoso Reservoir Loop
name_original: Siguiendo el río Mijares - circular por el Embalse de Arentes
description: This moderate 37.7-mile (60.7 km) road cycling route follows the Mijares River around the Arentes Reservoir, offering scenic views and a bal
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Albentosa are primarily defined by the Vía Verde Ojos Negros, a greenway built on a former mining railway line, offering a unique cycling experience. The region features varied landscapes, including mountainous terrain, river valleys, and pine forests, with notable structures like the Albentosa Viaduct and Tunnel. This area provides a mix of gentle gradients along the Vía Verde and more challenging climbs in the surrounding hills. Best road cycling routes around Albentosa The most popular road cycling route is Albentosa - Arenoso Reservoir Loop, a 37.7 miles (60.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 8 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the local reservoir and surrounding natural areas. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Arcos de las Salinas – Source of the Arcos River loop from Sarrión, a difficult 63.4 miles (102.0 km) path. This route traverses varied terrain, including climbs through the mountainous landscape towards the source of the Arcos River. Local road cyclists also love the Glorious poppy field (May) – View of Rubielos de Mora loop from El Casucho, a 41.3 miles (66.5 km) trail leading through scenic poppy fields and offering views of Rubielos de Mora, often completed in about 3 hours 13 minutes. Road cycling around Albentosa is defined by the Vía Verde Ojos Negros, impressive viaducts, and tunnels, set amidst mountainous terrain and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate greenway sections to more difficult climbs. The routes in Albentosa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 7 reviews. More than 600 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Albentosa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Albentosa?
A: Albentosa offers a diverse selection of nearly 30 road cycling routes. These routes cater to various skill levels, with a good mix of moderate and difficult options.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Albentosa?
A: The region around Albentosa generally offers pleasant conditions for road cycling during spring and autumn. These seasons typically provide milder temperatures, ideal for enjoying the varied landscapes without the intense heat of summer or the colder temperatures of winter. The 'Glorious poppy field (May) – View of Rubielos de Mora loop' suggests May can be particularly scenic.

Q: What are the typical terrain and difficulty levels for road cycling in Albentosa?
A: Road cycling in Albentosa features varied terrain. The Vía Verde Ojos Negros offers largely gentle gradients, often downhill when ridden towards the coast, making it accessible. However, the surrounding mountainous landscapes also provide more challenging climbs. Out of the nearly 30 routes, 11 are considered moderate and 17 are difficult, indicating options for different fitness levels.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the cycling routes in Albentosa?
A: Yes, the routes feature several impressive landmarks and offer scenic viewpoints. A highlight is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2614427" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albentosa Viaduct&lt;/a&gt;, an impressive structure with seven semi-circular arches offering panoramic views of the Albentosa River and the town. Cyclists also pass through the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4374269" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albentosa Tunnel&lt;/a&gt;, the longest on the Vía Verde. Other natural sights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4374209" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fuente de la Salud&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4374257" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the Linares Gorge&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What makes the Vía Verde Ojos Negros special for road cyclists in Albentosa?
A: The Vía Verde Ojos Negros is a primary draw for road cyclists in Albentosa. It's built on a former mining railway line, offering a unique, traffic-free cycling experience. It's one of Spain's longest greenways, largely downhill when ridden from the interior towards the coast, making it accessible for various fitness levels. It also features impressive engineering like the Albentosa Viaduct and Tunnel.

Q: Are there options for easier, beginner-friendly road cycling routes?
A: While many routes are rated moderate to difficult, the Vía Verde Ojos Negros itself provides sections with gentle gradients, making it suitable for beginners or those seeking a more relaxed ride. These sections are largely traffic-free, offering a comfortable and safe environment for less experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there challenging climbs for experienced road cyclists?
A: Absolutely. Beyond the gentle gradients of the Vía Verde, the region around Albentosa features impressive mountainous terrain. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31440893" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Arcos de las Salinas – Source of the Arcos River loop from Sarrión&lt;/a&gt;, rated difficult, traverse varied terrain with significant climbs, providing a demanding experience for seasoned road cyclists.

Q: Is there parking available for cyclists near the routes in Albentosa?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in and around Albentosa, especially near access points to the Vía Verde Ojos Negros. Specific parking information for individual trailheads can often be found on komoot route pages or local tourism resources.

Q: Can I access the cycling routes using public transport?
A: While Albentosa is a smaller village, it is connected by regional bus services. However, public transport options directly to specific trailheads for cycling might be limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules and routes in advance if you plan to rely on public transport for access.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Albentosa are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e962154820/albentosa-arenoso-reservoir-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Albentosa - Arenoso Reservoir Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20136816" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Glorious poppy field (May) – View of Rubielos de Mora loop from El Casucho&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about their experience in Albentosa?
A: The road cycling routes in Albentosa are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 7 reviews. Over 600 road cyclists have explored the area using komoot, often praising the unique experience of the Vía Verde Ojos Negros and the diverse, scenic landscapes.

Q: Are the road cycling routes around Albentosa suitable for families?
A: The Vía Verde Ojos Negros, with its traffic-free and largely gentle gradients, is particularly well-suited for families. It provides a safe and enjoyable environment for cyclists of all ages to experience the region's natural beauty. More challenging routes might be better for families with older, more experienced riders.

Q: What kind of natural landscapes can I expect to see while cycling?
A: Cyclists in Albentosa will encounter a rich variety of natural landscapes. The routes traverse impressive mountainous terrain, offering scenic backdrops. You'll also pass through serene pine forests, follow picturesque riverbeds, and experience the unique geological features of gorges and valleys. The Vía Verde itself winds through diverse scenery, from arid highlands to greener sections.
tips: Area of cultivated fields after the town of Venta de Aire in the direction of Los Pertegaces. Throughout the warm months they are barren crop fields, but in spring times, when there is water, an array of colorful wild plants appear.
Puebla de Arentes is a town located at the top of the Arenoso reservoir. The road winds over the body of water.
The landscape of Vilanova de Viver is mountainous and steep, and ravines and pine forests abound. The town is located 900 meters above sea level.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular route is approximately 60.7 km (37.7 miles) long and typically takes around 3 hours and 9 minutes to complete by racebike, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate. While the area around the Arenós Reservoir is known for challenging climbs, this specific route has an elevation gain of approximately 881 meters (2,890 feet), making it a good challenge for cyclists with good fitness.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily designed for racebikes, suggesting mostly well-paved surfaces. However, some segments in the broader region might include unpaved sections, so be prepared for varying road conditions. You'll likely encounter quiet minor roads, but some parts could be on regional roads.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The autumn and spring seasons are highly recommended for cycling this route. During these times, the natural scenery, particularly the black poplars and elms along the riverbanks, offers vibrant colors. Weather conditions are also generally more favorable for cycling.

faqs.question: Are there any notable viewpoints or natural features along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers impressive landscapes with mountains and rocky slopes. You can enjoy a View of Puebla de Arenoso. The Arenós Reservoir itself is a central feature, known for its beautiful turquoise waters. In May, you might even see a Glorious poppy field.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking for this exact route isn't detailed, the area around the Arenós Reservoir and Montanejos generally offers parking options. It's advisable to look for parking in nearby settlements like Villanueva de Viver or Montanejos, which are common starting points for routes in the region.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for racebikes on roads, which typically means it's not ideal for dogs to accompany cyclists directly on the pavement. If you plan to bring a dog, consider if they can safely travel in a bike trailer or if there are alternative walking paths nearby where they can be on a leash.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads and trails in this region. However, always respect local regulations and private property signs.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with sections of other official trails. You will encounter parts of the La Conquista de Valencia (15% of the route), [CIMA TE07] Fuente de Rubielos (6%), and the Camino del Cid (La Conquista de Valencia E05) (5%).

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or accommodation nearby?
faqs.answer: The town of Montanejos, famous for its hot springs, is a key hub in the area and offers various amenities including cafes, pubs, and accommodation. Other smaller settlements like Villanueva de Viver may also have limited services.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and elevation, you should bring appropriate cycling gear, including a helmet, water bottles, snacks, a repair kit, and navigation tools. Sun protection is also advisable. Consider layers of clothing, especially during spring and autumn, as temperatures can vary.
